coredata.dc:title: A “turn-on” fluorescent probe for glutathione detection based on the polyethylenimine-carbon dots-Cu2+ system
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Boye
Duan, Qianqian
Li, Yi
Zhang, Yixia
Che, Mingxuan
Zhang, Wendong
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) plays critical roles in many physiological processes usually present in live cells, and altered levels have been linked to some clinical pathological conditions. However, current techniques of GSH detection with fluorescence assay strategies remain poorly researched. In this work, branched polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) are synthesized by simple hydrothermal treatment of glucose and PEI. The fluorescence of the PEI-CDs could be efficiently quenched by Cu2+ and then recovered by some biothiols. Basing on this, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe for detecting GSH has been developed using PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system. Compared with traditional probes for GSH detection, a significant advantage of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system is that it can be used for GSH detection at both low and high concentrations with different concentration combinations of PEI-CDs and Cu2+. More specifically, two good linear relationships are achieved in the ranges of 0–80 μM and 0–1400 μM for GSH, respectively. Correspondingly, the detection limits of GSH are 0.33 μM and 9.49 μM, respectively. The quantum yields (QYs) of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-Cu2++GSH was 9.6% and 4.2%, respectively. Moreover, the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ has excellent optical stability and good biocompatibility. Additionally, it is worth noting that the developed probe has successfully realized the visualization of GSH detection in MGC-803 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system
Fluorescence probes
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of GSH by the Turn-on Fluorescence of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ System
The Reversibility of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ Used for GSH Detection
Selectivity of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ System towards GSH Detection
Application of the Fluorescent PEI-CDs-Cu2+ in Bioimaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PEI-CDs (inset: Particle size distribution of PEI-CDs). (b) FTIR spectrum of PEI-CDs. (c) Fluorescence spectra of PEI-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (d) The relationship between fluorescence and concentration of PEI-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the entire process of the GSH detection (inset: photographs of PEI-CDs, PEI-CDs-Cu2+, and PEI-CDs-Cu2+ +GSH solutions taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light, respectively). (b) UV absorption spectra of PEI-CDs, PEI-CDs-Cu2+, [Cu(NH3)4]2+, and PEI-CDs-Cu2++GSH solutions.

Fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ in the presence of different concentrations of GSH under 365 nm excited (a: detection system 1, b: detection system 2) (inset: the dependence of F/F 0 on the concentration of GSH). F: the recovered fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ solution containing GSH; F 0: the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ solution at 480 nm in the absence of GSH.

The reversibility of fluorescence by alternately adding equal volume of Cu2+ (1 mM) and GSH (1 mM) (inset: photographs of PEI-CDs solution by alternately adding Cu2+ and GSH taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light, respectively).

Fluorescence emission intensity of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system (detection system 1) with the absence (Blank) or presence of various amino acids (L-Thr, Arg, Glu, His, Ala, Asp, Leu, Lys, AA, Na2S, Hcy, Cys 1, GSH: 1 mM; Cys 2: 0.1 mM; Cys 3: 0.01 mM).
The fluorescence microscopy images of MGC-803 cells after incubated with PEI-CDs (a–c) and PEI-CDs-Cu2+ (d–f) for 6 h at 37 °C and 150 μM GSH for about 0.5 h (g–i).
Schematic illustration of the mechanism for fluorescence “turn-on” detection of GSH based on PEI-CDs.

head.ce:title: A “turn-on” fluorescent probe for glutathione detection based on the polyethylenimine-carbon dots-Cu2+ system

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Applied Mechanics and Biomedical Engineering & National Demonstration Center for Experimental Mechanics Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Applied Mechanics and Biomedical Engineering & National Demonstration Center for Experimental Mechanics Education, College of Mechanics, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong, 030600, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) plays critical roles in many physiological processes usually present in live cells, and altered levels have been linked to some clinical pathological conditions. However, current techniques of GSH detection with fluorescence assay strategies remain poorly researched. In this work, branched polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) are synthesized by simple hydrothermal treatment of glucose and PEI. The fluorescence of the PEI-CDs could be efficiently quenched by Cu2+ and then recovered by some biothiols. Basing on this, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe for detecting GSH has been developed using PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system. Compared with traditional probes for GSH detection, a significant advantage of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system is that it can be used for GSH detection at both low and high concentrations with different concentration combinations of PEI-CDs and Cu2+. More specifically, two good linear relationships are achieved in the ranges of 0–80 μM and 0–1400 μM for GSH, respectively. Correspondingly, the detection limits of GSH are 0.33 μM and 9.49 μM, respectively. The quantum yields (QYs) of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-Cu2++GSH was 9.6% and 4.2%, respectively. Moreover, the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ has excellent optical stability and good biocompatibility. Additionally, it is worth noting that the developed probe has successfully realized the visualization of GSH detection in MGC-803 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a central component of the redox homeostasis of cells that makes it a highly sensitive indicator of cell functionality and viability [1–3]. Moreover, the GSH level links to a number of human disease pathologies including cancer, cardiovascular, and neurodegenerative diseases [4–6]. Therefore, many kinds of technologies for GSH detection have been developed in the past few years. Among these detection technologies, fluorescence probe technology reveals a promising way for its real-time, high luminescence, as well as highly sensitive and photostability properties [7,8]. Thus various kinds of fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of GSH have been designed [9–11].

Nevertheless, these fluorescent probes are still unsatisfactory due to some disadvantages. For example, most studies are only effective and sensitive in one concentration region for one probe [12,13], and the study of GSH concentration in human tissues is still not extensive [14]. For example, the concentration of GSH in blood plasma is 4.9–5.9 μM, compared with 400–600 μM in epithelial cells and 500–1000 μM in whole blood [15,16]. Considering the large concentration differences, it may need two probes to detect GSH concentrations in blood plasma and whole blood respectively. Probes that are suitable for both low and high concentration ranges are sorely lacking. Moreover, poor reversibility also restricts the applications.

During the last few years, many fluorescent probes based on carbon dots (CDs) for detecting various biothiols, including cystein (Cys), homocystein (Hcy) and GSH, have been developed [17,18]. CDs, a new type of fluorescent nanoparticles, have received considerable attentions for their unique advantages such as small size, ease of functionalization, low cytoxicity, strong penetrability, and high chemical and optical stability in comparison to semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent agents [19,20]. The electronic structure and fluorescence of CDs can be effectively modified by doping hetero atoms to the p-conjugated system or amino-functionalized [21,22]. Branched polyethylenimine CDs (PEI-CDs) have an excellent fluorescence activity, and could be applied in chemical probes to recognize multiple analytes [21].

It is noteworthy that PEI can selectively capture trace-level free Cu2+ [22]. In addition, Cu2+ has stronger binding preference of some biothiols than PEI-CDs [23]. Thus, in this work, a fluorescent “turn-on” probe for detection of GSH based on the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system was developed. As shown in Scheme 1 , in the absence of GSH, the fluorescence of the fluorescent probes was very weak owing to the high quenching ability of Cu2+. However, the fluorescence of the fluorescent probes was recovered in the presence of GSH due to the stronger binding preference of GSH to Cu2+. Therefore, the “turn-on” fluorescence system involving fluorescence quenching by Cu2+ and subsequent fluorescence recovery upon addition of GSH makes it possible to detect GSH. The probe based on the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system could achieve precision measurements in different GSH concentration ranges by simply adjusting the solution concentrations of PEI-CDs and Cu2+. Additionally, the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system is potential to visualize the intracellular GSH level in living cancer cells.

Glucose, glycol, PEI, CuSO4, ammonia, ascorbic acid (AA), GSH and all other amino acids were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations were prepared by diluting the standard stock solution with ultrapure water. Human gastric cancer MGC-803 cells were provided by the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium and other reagents used for cell culture were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water purified by a Millipore Milli-Q system was used throughout the experiments.

The morphological characteristics of PEI-CDs were measured by a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-2100F, Tokyo, Japan). The surface functional groups of samples were performed using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Bruker Optik GmbH Tensor 27, Ettlingen, Germany). The absorption spectra were obtained from a UV–vis spectrophotometer (METASH UV8000, Shanghai, China). The fluorescence spectra were measured by Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Edison, USA). All images of cells were observed by fluorescence microscope (Leica DM3000, Wetzlar, Germany).

The PEI-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment [21]. Firstly, 0.5 g glucose was dissolved in 25 mL mixed solution of glycol/ultrapure water (v:v/1:1) to form a clear solution. The mixture was then transferred into a 50 mL teflon lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C in a constant-temperature drying oven for 3 h to form bare CDs. Finally, 1.0 mL PEI solution was added into 25 mL bare CDs solution and reacted for 4 h at 75 °C to form the PEI-CDs. The PEI-CDs solution turned to dark yellow and emitted strong yellow luminescence under ultraviolet lamp. 900 μL PEI-CDs solution was diluted 20 and 40 times to use as stock solutions, respectively. Then, 100 μL Cu2+ solution (1 mM and 100 μM, respectively) was added to the above stock solutions to constitute detection system 1 and 2, respectively. As described above, the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ detection system for GSH detection used in experiment was prepared. The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded at room temperature.

Human gastric cancer MGC-803 cells were seeded in a 24-well plate and grown in a cell culture incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C in RPMI 1640 culture medium. Cells were then treated with one of the following protocols: (1) After the cells reached 80% subconfluency, the culture media was replaced with fresh media and treated with PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-Cu2+ solution (detection system 1) for 6 h. Then, the medium was removed and the cells washed with fresh 1× PBS three times. The cells were observed under fluorescence microscopy; (2) the cells were treated with the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ solution (detection system 1) for 6 h. Then, the medium was removed and the cells washed with fresh 1× PBS three times. Next, the cells were then supplemented with GSH in the growth medium for another 0.5 h at 37 °C. Finally, the cells were observed under fluorescence microscopy.

The synthesized PEI-CDs have a uniform dispersion with an average size of 3.6 nm (Fig. 1a). According to the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b), the peaks at 1079 cm−1 and 2933 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of COC and the scissor vibration of CH, respectively. Moreover, scissor vibration of NH2 (1628 cm−1) and stretching vibration of NH2 and OH (3414 cm−1) were also observed, which indicated the surface of PEI-CDs was rich in NH2 groups. The fluorescent emission peak of PEI-CDs presented redshift from 475 to 565 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength (Fig. 1c). The maximum emission intensity was obtained under 365 nm excitation. The wavelength-dependent phenomenon maybe ascribed to the different particle sizes and different emission sites of the synthesized PEI-CDs [21]. To define the best diluted concentration, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs at different concentrations was further measured. As depicted in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs solution began to increase and reached its maximum value at the 20 times diluted concentration, and then gradually decreased with further dilution. This may be attributed to the fact that the distance between the PEI-CDs particles was very short when the PEI-CDs solution concentration was too high, resulting in concentration quenching.

Fig. 2a showed the fluorescence emission spectra of the entire GSH detection process by the developed system. Obviously, PEI-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescence emission band centered at 480 nm. As the Cu2+ was added, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly. However, with the addition of GSH into the mixture of PEI-CDs-Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity was recovered apparently. The quantum yields (QYs) of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-Cu2++GSH was 9.6% and 4.2%, respectively (ESM Fig. S1 and Table. S1; Fig. S2 and Table. S2). Additionally, the fluorescence emission intensity and peak position remained stable in a wide range of pH from 2 to 12, indicating excellent optical stability for biomedical applications requiring a variety of specific pH buffers (ESM Fig. S3).

In order to further confirm the mechanism for the GSH detection, the absorption spectra of PEI-CDs, PEI-CDs-Cu2+, [Cu(NH3)4]2+, PEI-CDs-Cu2++GSH solutions were recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 2b, compared with the absorption spectra of PEI-CDs, a new absorption band from 550 nm to 650 nm appeared for the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ solution, which represented the formation of cupric amine complexes as well as in the [Cu(NH3)4]2+ solution. This directly proved the reaction between Cu2+ and the surface amino groups on PEI-CDs, leading to the formation of the cupric amine complexes and resulting in the fluorescence quenching. Then, the absorption band ranged from 550 to 650 nm obviously disappeared upon addition of GSH, indicating the dissociation of cupric amine complexes. These results further suggested that the binding ability between GSH and Cu2+ is much stronger than that of the PEI-CDs and Cu2+. Thus, PEI-CDs will be released from the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ complex in the presence of GSH, which achieved the GSH detecting function. In other words, the Cu2+ cannot lead to the permanent destruction of the surface fluorescent structure of the PEI-CDs and the terminal amino groups with trapped Cu2+ could be recovered to the original state. Based on the ability of the GSH to recover the fluorescence, the “turn-on” fluorescent probe for GSH detection was developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH by the Turn-on Fluorescence of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ System
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the feasibility of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system for the detection of GSH, the linear response range of the probe was explored. As depicted in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system was sensitive to the concentration of GSH, and gradually recovered in a dose-dependent manner upon GSH addition. The value of F/F 0 had a good linear relationship with the GSH concentration in the range of 0–1400 μΜ (Fig. 3a inset). The sensitivity of two linear fitting curves was 0.0014 and 0.0025/uM, respectively, and the detection limit was calculated to be 9.49 μM.

The amount of trace-level free Cu2+ captured by PEI-CDs is dependent on the amount of the surface amino groups. And the GSH required for recovery of the fluorescence was related to the concentration of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system. Basing on this point, the PEI-CDs solution was futher diluted and used to obtain a more sensitive detection in a small concentration range. When the GSH concentration was in the range of 0–80 μΜ (Fig. 3b inset), a good linear relationship also was achieved. The sensitivity and the detection limit were promoted to 0.04/μM and 0.33 μM, respectively. It should be noted that the obtained detection limit of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ probe of GSH was almost equivalent to those previously reported fluorescent probes [24–26]. However, compared with those previous reports, the developed probe can be used for GSH detection at both low and high concentrations with different combinations of PEI-CDs and Cu2+ [24–26]. Therefore, it is believed that higher sensitivity and detection limit can be obtained in different GSH levels by optimizing the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system to meet the testing requirements under different conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Reversibility of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ Used for GSH Detection
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of photochromic chemical bond rearrangement, a certain degree of side reaction cannot be avoided, which limits the reversible reaction cycles. Reversibility (also known as fatigue resistance) is one of the important evaluating indicators in “turn-on” fluorescent probes [27]. As shown in Fig. 4 , when Cu2+ and GSH was alternately added to the PEI-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity could be switched for at least 5 cycles continuously with only a little bit fatigue. It showed the potential to be reusable with further optimization or calibration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ System towards GSH Detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of this method with the presence of other amino acids, the fluorescence responses of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system after adding 100 μL of different kinds of amino acids, ascorbic acid (AA), and Na2S were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , GSH, Cys, and Hcy showed remarkable “turn-on” effect on the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system, whereas no significant effect was observed in the presence of other amino acids, AA, and Na2S. The fluorescence intensity recovery efficiency of Cys is about 60% of that of GSH under the same concentrations (as denoted Cys 1). When the Cys concentration was reduced to 10 times (Cys 2) or 100 times (Cys 3) lower, the fluorescence intensity declined sharply. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity recovery efficiency of Hcy is about 30% of that of GSH. These results indicate that the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system has a high selectivity for GSH detection even with the presence of other biological thiols containing mercapto groups. In addition, it is worth mentioning that although both of GSH, Cys, and Hcy can turn on the quenched fluorescence, the intracellular level of GSH in mammalian cells is significantly higher than Cys and Hcy. For example, the concentration of Cys is 0.02–0.1 mM in human liver, but the concentration of GSH is 5–10 mM [28,29]. The concentration of GSH is about 100 times higher than Cys in human liver. Therefore, it is believed that the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system could be a promising probe for the intracellular GSH detection with high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the Fluorescent PEI-CDs-Cu2+ in Bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we carried out an in vitro bioimaging study using human gastric cancer MGC-803 cells as a model. After MGC-803 cells were incubated with PEI-CDs-Cu2+ in culture medium for 6 h and washed with fresh 1× PBS three times, the fluorescence was observed from within the cell because of the content of GSH in the cells (Fig. 6d–f). The cells were then supplemented with GSH in the growth medium for another 0.5 h at 37 °C, and the fluorescent intensity obviously increases (Fig. 6g–i). This may be because cellular uptake of the added GSH.

Considering the safety of potential biomedical applications, the cytotoxicity of PEI-CDs-Cu2+ was evaluated by the MTT assays on MGC-803 cells (Fig. S4). Results showed that the probe had no significant cytotoxic effect on MGC-803 cells. These results demonstrate that the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ has good biocompatibility, indicating that the prepared PEI-CDs-Cu2+ can be used as fluorescence probe for visualize the GSH inside living cells.

A “turn-on” fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of GSH was developed based on the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system. The probe could realize detection of different concentration ranges of GSH by optimizing the concentration ratios of PEI-CDs and Cu2+. As an example, small and large concentration ranges of 0–80 μM and 0–1400 μM for GSH could be detected easily with the detection limits of 0.33 μM and 9.49 μM, respectively. Additionally, the PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system can be utilized as a fluorescent probe for GSH detection in living cells. The PEI-CDs-Cu2+ system has promising GSH-related applications in quality control, clinical pharmacological research, biological imaging and disease diagnosis.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51622507, 51505324, 81602506, 61471255, 61474079), Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths (201701D221111), Scientific and Technologial Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (201802036, 2016143).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2019.111532.
